questions.prompt: What's gigacasting and what does it replace?

choices.text: High-pressure die casting at automotive scale: one ~80kg aluminum casting replaces ~70 stamped-and-welded parts (e.g. Model Y rear underbody). Eliminates the equivalent stamping operations, welds, inspection points, and BOM complexity. Structural cost reduction, not incremental.

questions.explanation: Gigacasting consolidates dozens of parts into one casting via high-pressure die casting with very large presses (6,000+ ton clamping force). Eliminates welds, simplifies assembly, reduces inspection points — a category change in body manufacturing, not just an optimization.

questions.prompt: A Tesla owner has a minor rear-end collision. Their vehicle is totaled. Why might gigacasting be relevant?

choices.text: Gigacast structural parts are harder to repair than welded structures — a crack typically means replacing the entire casting. Minor damage that would have been a fender repair on a traditional body can total a gigacast vehicle. The production cost win comes with a repair-cost externality borne by insurers and owners. Insurance premiums on some Tesla models reflect this.

questions.explanation: Gigacasting's tradeoff: lower production cost, higher repair cost. Cast parts can't be welded back together like steel panels. Insurance and consumer total-cost-of-ownership reflect this. It's the externality of the production innovation.
